responses.strategic_rationale: The plan confronts core tensions among scale, data fidelity, ethics, and funding. Five levers translate the Bonn residential parasomnia program from an initial eight-suite pilot into a scalable research platform for method validation and phenotyping. Adaptive footprint reduces upfront capital while preserving safety; tiered sensing broadens data richness without sacrificing privacy; recruitment and governance balance representativeness with consent overhead; and a diversified funding portfolio lowers financial risk while enabling translational work. Together, these levers enable iterative learning, controlled expansion, and eventual interventional studies aligned with Aim 2 and Aim 3, all within regulatory and ethics constraints.

levers.name: Adaptive Facility Footprint Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Eight suites activated with modular renovation baseline → Systemic: 25% faster scaling through pre-approved modular components and vendor agreements → Strategic: Enables rapid expansion while trading off initial safety checks and calibration time.
options: Maintain eight dedicated suites with fixed capacity and milestone-driven expansion, deferring capital investment until data quality and safety thresholds are met.
Scale to twelve suites with phased renovations and staged hiring, expanding only after throughput and annotation targets are demonstrated.
Adopt modular, co-located micro-facilities connected via federated analytics and a data-as-a-service partnership to enable rapid scaling and shared governance.
levers.review_lever: Trade-off: Capacity acceleration vs safety and data quality. Weakness: The options fail to consider interoperability with hospital information systems and long-term maintenance costs.

levers.name: Sensor Suite Architecture Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Initiate dry-EEG/mattress-sensor deployment → Systemic: 25% faster data processing throughput through richer datasets and streamlined workflows → Strategic: Elevates data fidelity while increasing privacy risk and device management complexity.
options: Limit sensing to on-site PSG nights with standard channels to minimize data management burden and maintain manual scoring.
Deploy tiered sensing including dry-EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, and occasional infrared video; escalate to full PSG nights on flagged events with clinician oversight.
Deploy edge-computing enabled sensing with federated analytics across Bonn and partner sites, enabling continuous real-time triage and a data-sharing marketplace.
levers.review_lever: Trade-off: Granularity vs privacy and intrusiveness. Weakness: Options fail to address potential data gaps due to sensor non-compliance or device failures.

levers.name: Ethical Recruitment and Retention Framework
levers.consequences: Immediate: Enrollment targets defined → Systemic: 20% higher enrollment pace and 15% lower attrition through flexible pathways and partner engagement → Strategic: Broadens representativeness and accelerates data accrual while maintaining ethical standards.
options: Enforce strict pre-admission pathway with limited channels, lengthy screening, and fixed stay windows to optimize data integrity.
Broaden recruitment through regional clinics, offer flexible stay durations, adaptive inclusion criteria, and structured participant engagement to reduce dropout.
Establish continuous enrollment via dynamic consent and national patient-network partnerships, supported by a participant-centric outreach flywheel and potential data-access revenue-sharing.
levers.review_lever: Trade-off: Enrollment velocity vs sample representativeness. Weakness: Options fail to consider inclusion of underrepresented groups and accessibility barriers.

levers.name: Governance and Data Stewardship Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Policy baseline for data governance established → Systemic: 25% faster data curation and inter-rater concordance through standardized pipelines → Strategic: Builds trust with participants and funders, enabling compliant data sharing while balancing privacy.
options: Maintain internal governance with fixed external advisory, local storage only, and de-identified data sharing under university DUAs.
Adopt EEG-BIDS pipelines, dual scoring QA, and deposits to recognized repositories; implement privacy-by-design for all platforms.
Pursue interoperable federated governance with privacy-preserving analytics and a data marketplace offering tiered access and revenue sharing across Bonn and partners.
levers.review_lever: Trade-off: Openness vs privacy and data ownership. Weakness: Options fail to consider regulatory shifts and data sovereignty across jurisdictions.

levers.name: Funding and Risk Portfolio Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Core grants secured with contingency budgeting → Systemic: 25% faster decision cycles through milestone-based releases and risk-aware planning → Strategic: Enables sustainable growth and translational potential while preserving scientific independence.
options: Pursue core DFG and University funding, maintain contingency buffers, defer industry partnerships unless essential.
Diversify funding with defined industry partnerships and staged grant renewals, maintain reserves, and align milestones with sponsor expectations.

Establish a data-access partnership model with industry and healthcare providers, monetizing de-identified datasets while retaining publications rights, paired with a Phase II interventional arm to demonstrate translational impact.
levers.review_lever: Trade-off: Ambition vs financial risk. Weakness: Options fail to consider long-term sustainability beyond three years and external funding volatility.

responses.summary: These levers span footprint, sensing, recruitment, governance, and funding, offering a coherent conservative-to-radical path while addressing speed, quality, privacy, and financial risk. A critical missing dimension is translational outcome planning; Add 'Phase II randomized interventional arm with behavioral/environmental modifications and a data-analytic framework to measure response' to Lever 5 to anchor translation and demonstrate real-world impact.

responses.strategic_rationale: The new levers broaden the strategic envelope beyond the initial Bonn parasomnia pilot by introducing a diffusion-ready footprint, federated sensing, dynamic recruitment, open governance scaffolding, and diversified funding. This portfolio enables cautious to radical evolution, balancing speed, data fidelity, ethics, and financial resilience while preserving scientific rigor and patient privacy. Each lever presents distinct pathways to scale, improve data quality, and sustain operations, with clear trade-offs between expansion tempo, governance complexity, and long-term sustainability. The combined framing supports iterative learning, cross-institution collaboration, and translational momentum toward future interventional work.

levers.name: Localized Modular Footprint Diffusion
levers.consequences: Immediate: Deploy eight suites with modular baselines → Systemic: 25% faster scaling through plug-in units and standardized procurement → Strategic: Expand while calibrating safety, interoperability, and long-term maintenance; trade-offs: speed versus risk.
options: Maintain eight suites with fixed capacity and milestone-driven expansion, deferring capital investment until data quality and safety thresholds are met.
Scale to twelve suites with phased renovations and staged hiring, expanding only after throughput and annotation targets are demonstrated.
Adopt modular, co-located micro-facilities connected via federated analytics and a data-sharing consortium to enable rapid scaling and unified governance.
levers.review_lever: Trade-off: Capacity acceleration vs safety and data quality. Weakness: The options fail to consider interoperability with hospital information systems and long-term maintenance costs.

levers.name: Federated Sensing and Privacy-First Architecture
levers.consequences: Immediate: Initiate tiered sensing with dry-EEG and mattress sensors → Systemic: 25% faster data curation through datasets and privacy-preserving processing → Strategic: Elevates data fidelity while increasing device management complexity and risk.
options: Limit sensing to on-site PSG nights with standard channels to minimize data management burden and maintain manual scoring.
Adopt tiered sensing including dry-EEG and mattress sensors; escalate to full PSG nights on flagged events with clinician oversight.
Implement edge-computing enabled sensing with federated analytics across Bonn and partner sites, enabling cross-site collaboration and risk-aware triage.
levers.review_lever: Trade-off: Granularity vs privacy and intrusiveness. Weakness: The options fail to address data gaps due to sensor non-compliance or device failures.

levers.name: Dynamic Enrollment and Retention Engineering
levers.consequences: Immediate: Expand enrollment pathways with flexible stays and dynamic screening → Systemic: 25% faster enrollment via consent optimization and partner networks → Strategic: Broader representativeness and faster data accrual with ethical safeguards.
options: Enforce strict pre-admission pathway with limited channels, lengthy screening, and fixed stay windows to optimize data integrity.
Broaden recruitment through regional clinics, offer flexible stay durations, adaptive inclusion criteria, and structured participant engagement to reduce dropout.
Implement dynamic consent with continuous engagement via digital channels and regional registries, enabling rapid multi-site enrollment while maintaining governance safeguards.
levers.review_lever: Trade-off: Enrollment velocity vs representativeness. Weakness: The options fail to consider inclusion of underrepresented groups and accessibility barriers.

levers.name: Open Governance and Compliance Scaffold
levers.consequences: Immediate: Establish baseline governance with oversight and local storage → Systemic: 25% faster data curation and inter-rater concordance via standardized pipelines → Strategic: Builds trust and enables data reuse while preserving privacy.
options: Establish baseline governance with oversight and local storage → Systemic: 25% faster data curation and inter-rater concordance via standardized pipelines → Strategic: Builds trust and enables data reuse while preserving privacy.
Adopt EEG-BIDS pipelines, dual scoring QA, and deposits to recognized repositories; implement privacy-by-design for all platforms.
Implement federated governance with privacy-preserving analytics and cross-institution data reuse under controlled access with a regional data consortium.
levers.review_lever: Trade-off: Openness vs privacy and data ownership. Weakness: The options fail to consider regulatory shifts and data sovereignty across jurisdictions.

levers.name: Diversified Financing and Risk Allocation
levers.consequences: Immediate: Secure core grants with contingencies and reserves → Systemic: 25% faster decision cycles via milestone-based releases and risk-aware buffers → Strategic: Enables sustainable growth and translational potential while preserving scientific independence.
options: Pursue core DFG and University funding, defer industry partnerships unless essential.
Diversify funding with defined industry partnerships and staged grant renewals, maintain reserves, align milestones with sponsor expectations.
Create a cross-sector data-sharing consortium with tiered access and sponsor-driven revenue sharing, while planning a Phase II interventional arm to demonstrate translational impact.
levers.review_lever: Trade-off: Ambition vs financial risk. Weakness: The options fail to consider long-term sustainability beyond three years and external funding volatility.

responses.summary: These levers extend footprint, sensing, recruitment, governance, and financing, creating a logically progressive path from conservative to radical. A missing dimension is translational intervention planning; Add 'Phase II randomized interventional arm with behavioral/environmental modifications and a data-analytic framework to measure response' to Lever 5 to anchor translation and funding momentum.

responses.strategic_rationale: The new levers broaden the strategic envelope into community governance, cross-institution data stewardship, participant welfare, annotation efficiency, and regulatory resilience. They enable cautious-to-radical evolution, balancing speed, data fidelity, ethics, and funding diversity while preserving scientific rigor and patient privacy for translational potential.

levers.name: Community-Driven Research Ecosystem Design
levers.consequences: Immediate: Establish community advisory board and dynamic consent pilots → Systemic: 25% faster enrollment and retention through trusted regional partnerships → Strategic: Enhances representativeness and sustained engagement, but increases governance overhead and consent fatigue.
options: Formalize community advisory board with fixed consent windows, robust privacy controls, and predictable engagement cadence.
Scale regional partnerships with dynamic consent pilots and flexible participation, expanding inclusivity.
Launch participatory data commons powered by trusted execution environments and blended funding to co-design studies.
levers.review_lever: Trade-off: Engagement speed vs privacy/governance overhead. Weakness: The options fail to consider interoperability with national ethics approvals and cross-border data flows.

levers.name: Cross-Institutional Data Stewardship Compact
levers.consequences: Immediate: Establish data-sharing terms with partner institutions → Systemic: 25% faster cross-site harmonization via common standards → Strategic: Expands collaboration and accelerates method validation, but increases legal/compliance overhead.
options: Limit data sharing to de-identified data within Bonn and current partner DUAs.
Define standardized data models and establish a lightweight cross-site collaboration framework with governance.
Create an inter-institutional data lake with TEEs-enabled cross-site analytics and blended funding for a researcher network.
levers.review_lever: Trade-off: Openness of data sharing vs compliance burden and data sovereignty. Weakness: The options fail to consider data sovereignty and vendor lock-in risk.

levers.name: Safety-First Longitudinal Participant Welfare Engine
levers.consequences: Immediate: Implement proactive welfare monitoring and rapid escalation triggers → Systemic: 25% reduction in adverse events and attrition through proactive support → Strategic: Improves ethical standing and data quality, but increases operating costs and staffing complexity.
options: Enforce strict safety protocols with fixed staffing ratios and minimal variance.
Add welfare liaisons and dynamic escalation policies with flexible stay windows.
Implement real-time welfare optimization using TEEs-enabled analytics and non-invasive sensors to guide clinician escalation and privacy-preserving interventions.
levers.review_lever: Trade-off: Participant safety vs. operating cost and staffing. Weakness: The options fail to consider caregiver burnout and long-term staff retention.

levers.name: Annotation Throughput and Validation Framework
levers.consequences: Immediate: Standardize scoring with dual raters and templates → Systemic: 25% faster annotation throughput via streamlined pipelines and calibration → Strategic: Improves reliability and publication quality, but increases training burden and scorer fatigue.
options: Maintain current scoring with two independent raters and adjudication.
Adopt semi-automated annotation aids and structured scoring templates.
Deploy TEEs-enabled triage with on-device tagging and cross-site annotation network.
levers.review_lever: Trade-off: Automation vs scorer accuracy. Weakness: The options fail to anticipate calibration drift and scorer turnover over long durations.

levers.name: Regulatory Agility and Risk Hedging Schema
levers.consequences: Immediate: Map EU and German regulatory requirements → Systemic: 25% faster compliance reporting via standardized templates → Strategic: Reduces risk of delays and sanctions, but elevates administrative overhead and governance coordination.
options: Maintain local compliance approach and limit external sharing.
Develop centralized compliance playbooks with cross-border governance and joint audits.
Create a regional compliance sandbox with TEEs-backed cross-jurisdiction data exchange and automated governance workflows.
levers.review_lever: Trade-off: Regulatory agility vs governance overhead. Weakness: The options fail to consider potential changes in funding landscapes and political risk.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate EXACTLY 5 levers per response. Do not generate more or fewer than 5 levers.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences MUST:
     • Chain three SPECIFIC effects: "Immediate: [effect] → Systemic: [impact] → Strategic: [implication]"
     • Include measurable outcomes: "Systemic: 25% faster scaling through..."
     • Explicitly describe trade-offs between core tensions
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional/innovative approach
     • Show clear progression: conservative → moderate → radical
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name levers as strategic concepts (e.g., "Material Adaptation Strategy")
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever`:
     • State the trade-off explicitly: "Controls [Tension A] vs. [Tension B]."
     • Identify a specific weakness: "Weakness: The options fail to consider [specific factor]."
   - For `summary`:
     • Identify ONE critical missing dimension
     • Prescribe CONCRETE addition: "Add '[full strategic option]' to [lever]"

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences
   - NO "[specific innovative option]" placeholders
   - NO value sets without clear strategic progression

6. **Option Structure Enforcement**
   - Radical option must include emerging tech/business model
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Establish a 3-year residential longitudinal research unit in Bonn, Germany, for the study of adult NREM parasomnias — sleepwalking, confusional arousals, and sleep terrors — with a secondary exploratory arm for REM sleep behavior disorder under a separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening, reflecting RBD's distinct clinical trajectory per current AASM guidance. The facility is affiliated with University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology, leveraging its existing long-term video-EEG monitoring culture, and addresses a specific methodological gap: traditional single-night polysomnography rarely captures infrequent parasomnia events, while outpatient actigraphy lacks physiological resolution. The unit houses 8 active sleep suites at launch in a converted residential property in a quiet Bonn neighborhood, renovated for safety (padded corridor edges, restricted-opening windows, silent exterior door alarms, fire safety, acoustic treatment, network cabling, accessibility modifications) while preserving a domestic feel — if the environment is too clinical, naturalistic behavior is suppressed. Physical capacity exists for 12 suites, but expansion is deferred until the pilot proves acceptable data quality, manageable false alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and usable annotation throughput. Sensing uses a tiered acquisition model: nightly low-burden monitoring via dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, unobtrusively mounted infrared cameras, ambient microphones, and environmental sensors; scheduled enhanced nights with fuller PSG montage including standard scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, and leg EMG on the first night, one mid-stay night, and the final night per participant; and escalation nights deploying full PSG after technician-flagged events to capture recurrence at clinical-grade resolution. All streams are time-synchronized and stored in EEG-BIDS-compatible format on a local NAS with nightly encrypted backup to university storage, annotated in real time by the night technician and later scored by two independent raters using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reported. Public data sharing is limited to de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements — bedroom video carries severe privacy constraints and is not deposited in public repositories. The scientific program has three explicit aims: Aim 1, establish and operationally validate the residential capture model as safe, ethics-approved, and sustainable; Aim 2, characterize within-person and between-person parasomnia patterns longitudinally, including episode frequency, morphology, triggers, and temporal clustering; Aim 3, develop and benchmark semi-automated event-triage tools that rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than promising autonomous diagnosis.

Participant recruitment targets adults aged 18–65 with NREM parasomnia confirmed through a structured pre-admission pathway: specialist clinical interview, collateral history from bed partner or household member where available, prior or screening polysomnography, and a structured exclusion checklist adjudicated by the PI. Explicit exclusions are nocturnal epilepsy or suspected epilepsy mimics, untreated obstructive sleep apnea, active major substance use disorder, severe psychiatric instability, and wandering or injury risk beyond facility capability. Recruitment channels are the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic, regional neurologist referrals, and the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network, with per-night compensation of €80 plus meals. A matched control group provides baseline nocturnal movement, arousal patterns, and false-positive sensor activity under identical residential monitoring conditions, enabling benchmarking of the event-triage tools against normal nocturnal behavior. Event-yield planning assumes that based on the inclusion criterion of at least 2 self-reported episodes per month, approximately 70–80% of admitted participants will produce at least one captured and adjudicated event during a 2–8 week stay; stays may be extended up to 2 additional weeks for participants with zero captured events after the initial period, capped at 10 weeks total, with a protocol ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions before triggering a recruitment-criteria review. The core team is 9 people: a PI who is a board-certified sleep medicine physician, 2 postdoctoral researchers in sleep neurophysiology and computational neuroscience respectively, 3 research technicians rotating night shifts to ensure safe coverage of 8 active suites with adequate margin for event response and annotation, a clinical psychologist specializing in sleep disorders, a data engineer managing the sensor pipeline, and a study coordinator handling recruitment, consent, scheduling, and ethics compliance.

Budget is €3.8 million over 3 years, gated: €1.8M year one covering property lease and renovation at approximately €750K, equipment at approximately €450K, staff hiring accounting for German social insurance contributions and actual overnight coverage, ethics approval, and a pilot cohort of 15–20 participants; €1.2M year two covering full operations with 30–35 participants, the enhanced-night PSG program, first publications, and event-triage algorithm development; €800K year three conditional on demonstrable progress by month 24. Personnel costs are approximately €2M over three years and remaining funds cover participant compensation, independent scoring time, data storage and retention, regulatory and legal work, and contingency. Funding sources are DFG research grant and University of Bonn internal research funding as the primary pillars, with a pharmaceutical industry partnership as optional upside rather than a feasibility requirement — if secured, the academic team retains full publication rights with the pharma partner receiving a 30-day pre-publication comment window only. Success criteria at 36 months: safe residential monitoring operations sustained for at least 24 months, enrollment of 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants plus matched controls, capture of a prespecified minimum number of independently adjudicated parasomnia events sufficient for Aim 2 phenotyping analysis, 2–3 peer-reviewed publications in sleep medicine journals covering methods validation and longitudinal characterization, a benchmarked semi-automated event-triage model with reported sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against dual human scoring, de-identified physiological dataset deposited in a recognized repository, and a submitted phase-two grant proposal for expansion and intervention studies. Failure triggers: if by month 18 the facility has enrolled fewer than 25 participants or captured fewer than 40 adjudicated events, the program undergoes external review. Governance is a 3-member external scientific advisory board reviewing progress every 6 months, with the PI holding operational authority and budget reallocations above €50K requiring board approval. Banned words: AI, blockchain, app, VR, AR, gamification, wearable startup, consumer product, DAO, NFT, metaverse, smart home, autonomous diagnosis, digital therapeutic, SaaS, marketplace, validated (in the context of claiming a mature deployable system). Pick a realistic scenario — this is an early-stage clinical research facility producing methods validation and phenotyping work, not a finished diagnostic platform.
